patient.name: Brandon Peretti
patient.chiefComplaint: Increasing somnolence and altered mental status over the past 24 hours

history.hpi: 48-year-old male, approximately 400 lbs (181 kg), with obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) and severe obstructive sleep apnea. Prescribed home BiPAP but rarely uses it. Over the past 24 hours his family reports he has been increasingly sleepy, difficult to rouse, with slurred speech. This morning he is arousable only to painful stimuli. No fever, no chest pain.
pastMedical: Chronic heart failure with preserved EF
history.events: Progressive somnolence over 24 hours. Family attempted to keep him awake with limited success. Now arousable only to pain.

scene.dispatch: Medic 9, respond code 3 to 2210 Pleasant Valley Road for a 48-year-old male, altered mental status, bariatric patient.
scene.location: Single-family home, main floor bedroom. Patient on a reinforced hospital-style bed in the living room (unable to climb stairs). Home BiPAP machine visible but unplugged.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Bariatric extrication and lifting will be required — call for bariatric transport and additional personnel.
scene.bystanders: Wife and adult son present, both knowledgeable about the patient's history and BiPAP equipment
visualCues: Morbidly obese male, approximately 400 lbs, supine in bed
Large neck circumference, short mandible — difficult airway predictors
Home BiPAP mask and machine on the nightstand, unplugged
Oxygen concentrator present but not running
Bed is a reinforced bariatric bed — patient cannot be moved to a standard stretcher easily
Daily medication organizer with some doses missed

phases.name: Arrival — Acute Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure
phases.description: Morbidly obese patient with likely acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure (OHS decompensation) from BiPAP non-compliance. Mental status is driven by CO2 narcosis. This patient has multiple difficult airway predictors — plan accordingly BEFORE you need to intubate.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent but with loud snoring respirations; large tongue and redundant pharyngeal tissue; jaw thrust improves
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Slow at 8/min, shallow; symmetric chest rise; clear or mildly diminished breath sounds bilaterally

skin.color: pink with central cyanosis around lips

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Withdraws to pain, no purposeful movement
otherFindings: Multiple difficult airway predictors: morbid obesity, large neck circumference, Mallampati IV, short thyromental distance, limited mouth opening
Presumed CO2 narcosis driving mental status
No signs of stroke or head trauma

expectedActions.action: Request bariatric transport and additional personnel; plan positioning and movement carefully
Position patient in ramped/sitting position with head elevated (reverse Trendelenburg ~30 degrees) — improves ventilation AND airway view if intubation becomes necessary
expectedActions.rationale: The ramped position is essential in obese patients — it relieves airway obstruction, improves FRC, and aligns the ear to sternal notch for laryngoscopy.

expectedActions.action: Jaw thrust with oropharyngeal airway (or NPA if gag intact); open the redundant pharyngeal soft tissue
Begin 2-person BVM with PEEP valve at 5-10 cmH2O; ventilate at 12 breaths/min to normalize CO2
Assess patient's ability to cooperate with BiPAP — if minimally rousable and respiratory effort exists, attempt BiPAP (IPAP 12/EPAP 5) as a trial; if no improvement in 5-10 minutes, prepare for RSI
expectedActions.rationale: BiPAP can dramatically reverse CO2 narcosis in OHS — but only if the patient has enough drive to cooperate. Worth a short trial before committing to RSI.

expectedActions.action: Establish IV access — may be difficult in a morbidly obese patient; have IO backup ready
Check blood glucose to rule out alternate cause of AMS
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography
Verbalize a difficult airway plan BEFORE needing it: primary VL attempt in ramped position, bougie, supraglottic airway backup, surgical airway kit at bedside, adequate preoxygenation

phases.name: BiPAP Trial
phases.description: Patient tolerates BiPAP with some improvement in mental status and ventilation. BiPAP is continued with intensive monitoring during transport. If mental status continues to improve, intubation is avoided.
phases.triggerCondition: Patient can tolerate BiPAP with respiratory effort preserved

transitions.condition: Mental status continues to decline on BiPAP

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent with BiPAP mask; snoring resolved
clinicalPresentation.breathing: BiPAP delivering consistent tidal volumes; respiratory rate normalizing
otherFindings: Patient beginning to follow simple commands

expectedActions.action: Titrate BiPAP settings as needed; target gradual CO2 decrease and improving mental status
Maintain ramped position during transport
Continuous mental status, SpO2, EtCO2 monitoring — be prepared to escalate if BiPAP fails
Transport to ED with bariatric capability; early notification
Do NOT administer sedatives for BiPAP anxiety — this patient's drive is already compromised

phases.name: Branch: Difficult Airway RSI
phases.description: BiPAP fails or the patient cannot cooperate. RSI is required despite multiple difficult airway predictors. Ramped position, video laryngoscopy, and backup plans are essential.
phases.triggerCondition: BiPAP fails or patient cannot cooperate

clinicalPresentation.airway: Snoring with OPA, BVM difficult due to body habitus
otherFindings: Poor BVM seal due to body habitus
Preoxygenation limited due to FRC issues — expect rapid desaturation

expectedActions.action: Confirm ramped position — head elevated with shoulders and occiput aligned; ear-to-sternal-notch alignment
Aggressive preoxygenation with BiPAP or 2-person BVM with PEEP 10 cmH2O for at least 3-5 minutes; apneic oxygenation via NC at 15 LPM
Administer Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV (dose based on IDEAL body weight ~72 kg, so 100-140 mg) — dosing on IBW in obese patients prevents overdose
expectedActions.rationale: Induction agents should be dosed on IBW or adjusted body weight in obesity, not total body weight, to avoid excessive effect.

expectedActions.action: Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV dosed on TOTAL body weight (181 kg → 180 mg rounded to available dose) — paralytics are dosed on TBW
expectedActions.rationale: Unlike induction agents, paralytics are dosed on total body weight to ensure adequate paralysis in the highly perfused obese patient.

expectedActions.action: Perform video laryngoscopy; have bougie in-hand and supraglottic airway/cric kit ready; single best-effort attempt, then rescue with SGA if unsuccessful
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, chest rise
Post-intubation ventilation with PEEP 10+ cmH2O, tidal volume 6-8 mL/kg IBW (not TBW), gradual CO2 correction
expectedActions.rationale: Rapid normalization of chronically elevated CO2 can cause alkalosis, seizures, and cerebral vasoconstriction. Correct slowly.

criticalActions: Position patient in ramped (head elevated) position
Trial BiPAP if patient retains respiratory drive and cooperation
Do NOT sedate for BiPAP anxiety
Verbalize difficult airway plan with backups BEFORE needing it
If RSI: ramped position, induction on IBW, paralytic on TBW, video laryngoscopy with bougie, SGA and surgical airway as backup
Post-intubation: gradual CO2 correction, PEEP 10+, TV on IBW
expectedActions: Continuous mental status monitoring during BiPAP trial
bonusActions: Articulate why induction and paralytic agents are dosed differently in obesity
Articulate the physiology of rapid CO2 correction complications
Recognize multiple difficult airway predictors and prepare accordingly

learningObjectives: Recognize OHS acute decompensation and CO2 narcosis
Apply a trial of NIV before committing to RSI in a cooperative patient
Understand the ramped position and its importance in obese patients
Dose induction agents on IBW and paralytics on TBW
Plan for a difficult airway before intubation is needed
discussionQuestions: Why is the ramped position so important in obese airway management?
How do you dose induction agents versus paralytics in obese patients?
What is CO2 narcosis and why does rapid correction cause harm?
Would BiPAP at home have prevented this decompensation?
When is it appropriate to give a trial of NIV in an obtunded patient?
commonPitfalls: Laying the patient flat and losing airway patency
Under-dosing paralytic in a large patient leading to inadequate paralysis
Over-dosing induction agents by using total body weight
Rushing to hyperventilate and causing cerebral vasoconstriction
Administering sedatives to improve BiPAP tolerance
keyTakeaways: Ramped position is the single most important intervention for the obese airway
NIV is worth a trial when the patient retains drive and cooperation
IBW for induction, TBW for paralytic
